data.title: Fans ecstatic after Michelle Yeoh reveals she owns over 100 Labubu dolls
data.description:
Oscar-winning Malaysian actress Michelle Yeoh melted hearts on the Tonight Show Starring Jimmy Fallon after sharing that she has more than 100 Labubu dolls, a secret number fans never saw coming.
Yeoh revealed that she has over 100 of them tucked away at home.
“Oh maybe a little more than a hundred of them,” she shared casually on the show, as if having a miniature mythical creature colony is completely normal.
Host Fallon, understandably gobsmacked, blurted out, “You have 100 Labubus?”
Without missing a beat, Yeoh confirmed she owned more than 100 Labubu dolls.
“Yeah, but they come in big and small, you know. You can’t just choose different colours,” she said.
The conversation unfolded after Fallon showed Yeoh a photo of her and her family, celebrating her birthday with a Labubu-themed cake.
“And you have Labubu cakes and this is before the trend happened.
“Michelle, you were ahead of the game. Do you love Labubus?” Fallon asked.
Yeoh admitted she did, then melted the studio with an adorable gesture: she gifted Fallon two Labubus for his daughters, featuring her initials M and Y.

Once the clip hit social media, fans could not contain themselves.
“She’s so sweet,” a user commented.
“The way she was so happy giving the mini Labubu to Jimmy,” wrote another.
A user added: “The best Labubu queen ever.”
This story was first published by The Star

data.title: Hong Kong’s take on Peking duck, China’s ancient Qing dynasty: 7 Lifestyle highlights
data.description:
We have selected seven Lifestyle stories from the past seven days that resonated with our readers. If you would like to see more of our reporting, please consider subscribing.
1. How top Hong Kong chefs make their Peking duck extra special
Marinated in a rich blend of Sichuan spices and roasted until the skin transforms into a crackling golden armour, the flaming Peking duck at Hutong restaurant in Hong Kong’s Tsim Sha Tsui neighbourhood is a feast for the senses.
2. Shameful period? How China’s Qing dynasty brought both wealth and humiliation

The Qing dynasty (1644-1912) was the last imperial dynasty of China. During the Qing, which lasted almost 270 years, China experienced unprecedented wealth and stability, and cosmopolitan culture flourished.
3. Wicked: For Good – Ariana Grande and Cynthia Erivo dazzle in Part 2

Jon M. Chu’s Wicked returns for its concluding part, following the sensational box office, 10 Oscar nominations and two wins of last year’s prequel.
4. He lost over 40kg in 7 months after pandemic food binge led to panic attack
Meir Ben Tovim’s wake-up call came in the middle of the night as he was struck with excruciating heartburn and struggled to breathe. He rushed to the hospital and was told he was having a panic attack.
5. 3 Hong Kong romance films that pushed the boundaries

Hong Kong romances are often melodramatic, and in the 1990s, increasingly geared towards teenagers. However, some filmmakers were still experimenting with the genre.
6. How climate change and extreme weather affect mental health
Hongkongers have experienced extreme weather recently. Super Typhoon Ragasa in late September was the second No 10 typhoon warning of the year, and the strongest tropical cyclone globally of the year at the time of its approach. A black rainstorm on August 5 lasted more than 11 hours and triggered torrential floods. This October was the hottest since records began in 1884.
7. Late-night tango in Hong Kong draws those ‘longing for human connection’

It is just before midnight on a weeknight, and the Fringe Club in Hong Kong is alive with a very different kind of energy from the rowdiness of nearby Lan Kwai Fong. Here, a group watches in awe as a polished couple dances the Argentine tango.

data.title: China’s exports may be targeted as US, EU consider tough rules of origin: economist
data.description:
China should prepare for stricter US-EU regulations on global rules of origin – as signalled in their recent trade deal – potentially weighing on its export sector by redefining global trade patterns, according to a Chinese economist.
“Trump’s current measures are temporary – tariffs can be imposed today and rolled back or even eliminated tomorrow. The real question for us is what the future global trade framework looks like,” said Zhang Yuyan, an academician at the Chinese Academy of Social Sciences.
Zhang said Western countries could introduce a more granular approach to rules of origin – the criteria used to determine where a product comes from, often for tariff purposes – turning it into a central pillar in shaping future trade.
“This is very important... The current rules of origin are relatively coarse,” Zhang said on Tuesday at the International Forum on China’s Economy and Policy in Hong Kong.
“China needs to be prepared for the likelihood that these rules could be directed at it,” he added, noting that about 60 to 70 per cent of the country’s exports are intermediate goods – often processed in third countries, complicating tariff enforcement.
A shift to more detailed value-added calculations along supply chains could lay the foundations for a more targeted tariff system, Zhang said.
A White House fact sheet on the US-EU trade deal, released in July, said the two sides would establish “strong rules of origin to ensure that the benefits of this agreement flow directly to the United States and the European Union, not to third countries”.
However, Zhang cautioned that even if Washington and Brussels move in this direction, intention does not automatically translate into implementation. Multiple rounds of negotiations would inevitably take place, while China’s comprehensive industrial chain puts it in a strong bargaining position, he added.
Zhang highlighted the rare earth industry as an example, noting that China’s advantage lies not in the size of its reserves but in its dominance across the entire supply chain – from refining to end-product manufacturing – giving Beijing leverage in trade talks with the US this year.
“Advanced economies may hold advantages in particular segments of the supply chain,” he said, citing as an example the lithography machines used to make semiconductors, the most advanced models of which are exclusively sourced from Europe.
“But China usually has the entire industry chain within its borders.”
“If others try to target China, Beijing can still use its strengths [to retaliate]: the breadth and completeness of its manufacturing ecosystem.”

data.title: Asia-Pacific investors brace for market correction in 2026 amid tech bubble fears: survey
data.description:
A majority of Asia-Pacific institutional investors believe markets are due for a correction in 2026, citing a potential tech bubble, geopolitical tensions and recession as their primary worries, according to a survey report published on Tuesday by Natixis Investment Managers.
Global markets had been “remarkably resilient” in 2025 despite challenges such as tariffs, geopolitical conflicts and supply chain disruptions, according to Natixis. However, 74 per cent of institutional investors globally – including 80 per cent in the Asia-Pacific region – believed a correction was overdue.
Among Asia-Pacific investors, 48 per cent cited the potential for a tech bubble as their top concern, followed by geopolitical shocks at 45 per cent and recession at 40 per cent. For global investors, geopolitical shocks ranked first, with 49 per cent expressing concern.
The sentiment suggested that “markets could run out of luck in the new year”, the asset-management firm said.
About 60 per cent of investors in the region planned to boost their allocations to equities within their home region to diversify away from the US.

“After a prolonged period of US market outperformance, international and non-US equities moved back into focus in 2025,” said Dora Seow, CEO of Natixis Investment Managers Singapore.
The survey “indicates that even as institutional investors prepare for another spell of heightened uncertainty in 2026, they remain both optimistic and pragmatic, seeking new sources of alpha in markets such as Asia-Pacific.”
Only 25 per cent of global investors planned to increase their allocations to US equities, compared with 44 per cent for Asia-Pacific equities and 42 per cent for equities in emerging markets.
Views on the Chinese market remained subdued, with three-quarters of global and Asia-Pacific investors indicating that slow growth was the “new normal”. However, Asia-Pacific investors displayed greater confidence in China’s resilience, with 70 per cent believing the country could withstand a prolonged trade war with the US.
About 70 per cent of investors globally and in the region anticipated that further deglobalisation would necessitate more region-specific strategies in emerging markets.
The survey also underscored the increasing prominence of alternative investments in portfolio allocation. Over 60 per cent of investors in the Asia-Pacific region and globally planned to increase their allocations to cryptocurrencies next year, expecting these new assets to outperform traditional stocks and bonds.
Currently, 26 per cent of Asia-Pacific investors held cryptocurrencies, the survey showed.
The findings were based on responses from 515 institutional investors – including public and private pension funds, insurers, foundations, endowments, central banks and sovereign wealth funds – that collectively manage about US$30 trillion in assets worldwide.
The Asia-Pacific markets surveyed included mainland China, Hong Kong, Taiwan, Japan, Singapore and South Korea.

data.title: Baidu founder Robin Li casts AI as the driver of China’s ‘new productive forces’
data.description:
Chinese search engine and artificial intelligence pioneer Baidu is casting AI as the engine of China’s push for “new productive forces”, touting the emerging technology as a linchpin of Beijing’s economic growth drive.
“AI, as a key driver of the new wave of technological and industrial transformation, is vital for accelerating the emergence of new quality productive forces and achieving high-quality economic and social development,” said Robin Li Yanhong, founder and chief executive at Beijing-based Baidu, in a bylined commentary published on Thursday in the state-run People’s Daily.
Beijing is currently rolling out its “AI Plus” strategy – a State Council initiative that aims to integrate AI across the economy and public services – in which China’s technology giants, including Baidu, are expected to play significant roles.
Baidu stock price gained 2.2 per cent in Hong Kong on Thursday morning to $HK113.6.
Li wrote in the opinion piece that Baidu was a key AI power in China, putting its own Ernie model family on par with DeepSeek, the Chinese AI start-up whose low-cost models have shaken up the global large language model market.
Baidu’s Ernie family – short for “Enhanced Representation through kNowledge IntEgration” – is the company’s flagship line of large language and multimodal models, powering its Ernie Bot chatbot and a range of cloud and enterprise AI services.
“Baidu … has the responsibility, the capability and the confidence to lead this transformation,” Li said, pledging to increase the company’s AI investment to develop frontier AI infrastructure and AI model technology.

The article came two days after Baidu reported a 50 per cent increase year on year in AI-related revenue in the three months ended September, a bright spot in an otherwise lacklustre quarter.
The company said sales at its AI-related cloud infrastructure, applications and marketing services rose to 10 billion yuan (US$1.4 billion), laying “a solid foundation for sustainable long-term growth”.
Li wrote in the commentary that AI could be applied across traditional sectors and business scenarios, from live streaming e-commerce, online sales and customer service to autonomous driving and assistive coding.
“Leveraging the power of AI could help enterprises cut costs, improve revenue, make better decisions and find new growth opportunities,” he said.
He called on “companies from thousands of industries and hundreds of sectors to internalise AI and develop AI-native capabilities”, saying wider adoption of the technology would help grow the “intelligence industry” and accelerate the upgrade of conventional sectors.
Baidu’s push comes amid intense competition in China’s AI market, where rivals such as Alibaba Group Holding – owner of the Post – and Tencent Holdings are also racing to build out their own tech stacks and commercialise large models.

data.title: China accuses US of undermining ties with Greece in envoy’s Piraeus port remarks
data.description:
China has accused the United States of trying to undermine its relations with Greece after the US ambassador Kimberly Guilfoyle urged Athens to sell the Chinese-operated port of Piraeus.
Guilfoyle called the presence of China’s state-owned shipping giant Cosco at the port in the south of Greece “unfortunate”, in an interview last week with Greek broadcaster Antenna TV.
“But I think there are ways around it, that something could be worked out, whether you pursue a path of enhancing output in other areas or perhaps that Piraeus could be for sale,” she said.
“I think it’s very important to have American infrastructure here to help support the region. To perhaps, in fact, enhance output from other ports and areas to balance against the Chinese influence with the port of Piraeus.”

The Chinese embassy in Greece responded on Wednesday, expressing Beijing’s firm opposition to the US envoy’s remarks which were full of “Cold War mentality and hegemonic logic”.
The interview had also revealed Washington’s intention to exploit the port to “serve its own geopolitical interests”, an embassy spokesperson said, adding that “the Piraeus port will always belong to the Greek people”.
“It is not a tool for the US to conduct geopolitical confrontation or undermine regional prosperity and stability, still less should it fall victim to geopolitics.”
Calling the port a fine example of commercial cooperation between Beijing and Athens, the embassy added that China would continue to increase its investment in the future.
Thanks to its strategically important geographical position, Piraeus is often described as the gateway to Europe for goods coming from Asia. Yet, when Greece sold the ancient port under pressure from the country’s European creditors in 2016, Cosco was the only bidder.
Since then, the Chinese shipping giant has secured a majority stake in the port, which is regarded as a vital part of the Belt and Road Initiative, Beijing’s massive global infrastructure project.
China invested heavily in Greece when the country was debt-ridden during its prolonged economic crisis, with the aim of boosting global Chinese trade as part of its belt and road plan.
Beijing has become an increasingly powerful player in Greece. Last year, the port of Piraeus achieved record revenues and profits, with its post-tax profit reaching 30 per cent year-on-year growth, according to China’s state news agency Xinhua.
Guilfoyle’s remarks were not the first time that Chinese-backed initiatives have come under attack from the US. In his inaugural speech in January, President Donald Trump declared that China was “operating” the Panama Canal and vowed to take it back.
Trump was referring to Hong Kong-based Hutchison Ports, which won the right in 2021 to operate two ports at the canal’s Atlantic and Pacific exits.
While Beijing was not directly involved in the venture, Panama came under increasing pressure from Washington, announcing in February that it was withdrawing from the belt and road scheme.


Earlier this year, CK Hutchison Holdings said it would sell a majority stake in its global ports business, which includes the Panama ports, to a US-led consortium. The deal was blocked by Beijing’s regulatory authorities.
Hutchison was reportedly discussing the inclusion of a Chinese investor in the consortium in an effort to smooth the deal.
South America’s first Chinese-funded port in Peru’s Chancay – another flagship joint project, this time between Beijing and Lima – also raised concerns in Washington when it was inaugurated in November last year.
The then US envoy for Latin America, Brian Nichols, called on countries in the region to be cautious about Chinese investment. At the same time, Trump’s transition team proposed imposing a 60 per cent tariff on all goods from the port.
Nearly a year later, China launched its first direct break-bulk shipping service to Chancay port in September, a move that steps up its trade, investment and influence in Latin America.

data.title: Cambodian beauty queen angers Thais over border row comment
data.description:
A Cambodian beauty queen has stirred controversy in Thailand after giving a political speech accusing Bangkok of provoking conflict during a pageant segment in Phnom Penh, with social media users accusing her of using an international dispute to grab attention.
A day after Chouri Laorhours was crowned Miss Teen Cambodia, her tearful speech was posted on the pageant’s social media account.
In a video, Laorhours carries a Cambodian flag and starts her speech in Khmer. Speaking “on behalf of all Cambodian people,” she asks for the return of 18 Cambodian soldiers being held by Thailand, according to the Khmer Times newspaper.
She then switches to English, adding: “Cambodia and Thailand have always lived together in peace, but now that peace has ended because Thailand started the war, something we never asked for … We do not want to fight. We want peace. We are not enemies. We are neighbours, human beings who deserve safety and a future free from hatred and war. Thank you.”
Soon after the clip was posted on Monday, it drew contrasting comments on both sides of the border.
Cambodians commended her for her patriotic speech. Thais, however, questioned the appropriateness of making such a political statement during a beauty pageant, while others accused her of fanning anti-Thai sentiments at such a sensitive time, The Nation reported. The phrase “Thailand started the war” is also trending online.
Tensions continue to rise between the two nations despite a US-brokered ceasefire agreement signed by them late last month.

On November 10, Thailand said it was suspending the deal and halting the release of 18 Cambodian soldiers in its custody after a landmine, said to have been recently planted by Cambodia, exploded and injured two Thai soldiers.
Since then, skirmishes have been reported, with the two sides accusing the other of shooting first.
Neither Thai officials nor the pageant organisers have issued a formal response to Laorhours’ speech, according to The Nation report.
Laorhours is no stranger to controversy. Throughout the campaign, she was criticised by social media users who said she was a perennial pageant queen because of her wealthy background, the Khmer Times reported.
She denied the accusations, saying that her achievements were the result of talent, experience and hard work.
“Those who think I win because I have money are wrong. I always compete based on my ability. The pageant chooses contestants who truly have talent. Whether someone has resources or not, if they have more ability, they will win the crown with dignity,” she posted on social media.
Beauty pageants have been under fresh scrutiny in Thailand, especially after negative reports about the ongoing Miss Universe pageant held in the kingdom.

Two judges have resigned after claiming the results were fixed, and Miss Universe Thailand director Nawat Itsaragrisil has been sidelined after he reportedly called Miss Mexico a “dumbhead”.
Miss Israel also said she received death threats after an edited video, purportedly showing her shooting a dirty look at Miss Palestine standing nearby, went viral.
“It’s not only death threats, but sexual assault threats,” Melanie Shiraz told the New York Post on Friday. “I experienced antisemitism before, but I didn’t think it would be this bad.”
Last month, Israel agreed to a deal with Hamas to end the Israel-Gaza war, but sporadic clashes continue to threaten the fragile peace.

data.title: ‘Unemployed penguin’: fans saddened by imminent retirement of Japan beloved smart card mascot
data.description:
The beloved penguin mascot of a transport card in Japan, regarded by many as the most popular penguin in the country, is expected to retire in 2027, according to its issuer.
The affable Suica Penguin will be hanging up its flippers at the end of the 2026 financial year, which concludes on March 31, 2027. It has graced the face of a widely used transport smart card since 2001, as announced by the Tokyo-based rail operator JR East on November 11, reported the Tokyo Weekender.
This card is the first transport Integrated Circuit (IC) card in Japan, with the name Suica derived from the acronym for Super Urban Intelligent Card.

The company revealed it has issued over 110 million such cards over the years, suggesting that nearly everyone in Tokyo has encountered this beloved penguin.
Locally, residents often refer to this card as the “watermelon card” because Suica sounds similar to the word for the fruit in Japanese.
Initially designed for train boarding, the card later evolved to include tapping functionality for payments at convenience stores.
As a result, the Suica Penguin has become the most recognisable cartoon image among tourists visiting Japan, thanks to the card’s extensive use.

This adorable penguin, an Adélie, a species from Antarctica, was created by Chiharu Sakazaki, a picture book artist and illustrator celebrated for her minimal yet charming and expressive style.
Sakazaki explained that the penguin was originally a character in her picture book before being discovered by the art director who developed the visual identity for the Suica card.
The penguin made its debut in posters promoting the card’s launch, and its overwhelming cuteness prompted the rail company to feature it on the card itself.
Since then, the penguin has gained such fame that it even has a statue located in Suica Penguin Square in Shinjuku, Tokyo.
Earlier this year, during a marketing event for the Suica card, the penguin’s belly was designed to protrude, inviting passers-by to touch it for stress relief. The company also stationed an employee near the penguin image to assist visitors in taking photographs.

JR East announced that a new character will take over, with plans to invite the public to participate in the selection process, although further details have yet to be revealed.
Many people have questioned the company’s decision, with some even petitioning for its reversal.
“I don’t think there’s any need for the penguin to ‘graduate.’ There must be some compelling reason behind this,” commented one internet user.
Another expressed sadness, saying, “I am heartbroken by this news. I will miss it…I feel the penguin on this card is becoming extinct, much like the dodo bird.”

A third user remarked: “I want the penguin to live on. Please don’t let Japan become less charming.”
A shopping website selling Suica Penguin merchandise experienced such high traffic that it took five minutes to access the site after the news of the mascot’s retirement broke.
The announcement also garnered attention on social media in China.
“I don’t understand why a mascot needs to retire. The penguin is so iconic. I feel sad about its retirement,” commented a mainland Chinese observer.

data.title: Inside the Hong Kong collectors’ society that treats its antiques like ‘babies’
data.description:
For Dr Maria Mok Kar-wing, staging an exhibition of more than 500 priceless items tickled her maternal instincts. “It was more like handling 500 babies,” says the director of the Hong Kong Museum of Art (HKMoA) of organising “Engaging Past Wisdom: Min Chiu Society at Sixty-five”. “The collectors are their parents and these treasures are their babies.”
Never mind that some of them are thousands of years old. The infants in question are, in fact, Chinese antiques, and their “parents” as such are members of the prestigious Min Chiu Society, a group of art collectors dedicated to the preservation and appreciation of classical Chinese arts and crafts.
Established in 1960 with 15 members including founding chairman Hu Jenmou, the Min Chiu Society intentionally operates under the radar. Besides its quinquennial exhibition, its collections and activities are not publicised.

“That is the intention: it’s a closed society, and we’re very low key,” says Lawrence Chan Wing-tat, chairman of the group. Passion for Chinese antiquities is a common factor among members, who come from various professional backgrounds and include industrialists, bankers, architects, doctors and lawyers. Chan himself has a background in advertising but is also known for his Dawentang Collection, which is highly regarded for its imperial porcelain from the Ming and Qing dynasties.
We’re seated in the museum’s conference room, sipping hot oolong tea from porcelain cups and looking out of the floor-to-ceiling windows at the uninterrupted view of Hong Kong Island across Victoria Harbour. It’s an unusual moment of reflection found in a typically fast-paced, anxiety-ridden Hong Kong day.
“Most of our activities are dedicated to learning about these antiques and preserving their history,” says Chan. “We’re intensely passionate about gaining knowledge.”

Bridging the gap between collecting and connoisseurship, real luxury is derived from appreciating an object’s exquisite craftsmanship and understanding the historical context in which it was conceived.
Hence the title, “Engaging Past Wisdom”, based on a Chinese idiom that translates to “learning from the past to understand the present”. The first character in the idiom is derived from a pictogram used in ancient China, representing a figure bent down over a basin of water looking at their own reflection.
The society has the kind of cultural cachet that results in its members being sought by institutions such as the Victoria and Albert and the British Museum in London and the Met in New York. And while on occasion they do lend their pieces to foreign institutions, the society’s relationship to the HKMoA in particular has stood the test of time.
“For Min Chiu collectors, they hold on to the belief that collecting is a passion; it’s not just about investment,” says Mok. “So when I compared the pieces to babies, I was joking, but not really. It’s something that they love and, for us, it’s about translating that love from the collector.”

Collecting art and belonging to the Min Chiu Society brought Chan and his wife together in more ways than one. The couple was introduced to the society by James Watt, a scholar and former curator at HKMoA.
“James travelled a lot to China in the 1960s and 70s and has many stories about the love of emperors for collecting antiquities,” says Chan. “I thought, well, if it was good enough for emperors, it’s good enough for me.”
The society’s clubhouse is now on Bowen Road and serves as a salon of sorts. Members convene for a monthly dinner at the premises, where they exchange thoughts, ideas and information. At times they invite academics, experts, museum curators and directors to speak. “In one simple flat you can see the collecting soft power that Hong Kong has,” says Mok. “It spans generations, too.”
The process to become a Min Chiu member is rigorous. While already knowing other members and having a deep interest in Chinese arts and culture are a given, potential candidates also need to undergo several rounds of meetings and interviews, and be vetted by a committee, whose decision needs to be unanimous.

Expanding and diversifying membership, however, is seen as key to cementing the society’s legacy and ensuring its scholarly work continues. Chan says women are now able to join the society, which has “definitely expanded the membership and visibility”.
Nurturing an appreciation among the public, particularly among the younger generations, for Chinese arts and antiquities is also high on the agenda. “We want the audience to walk away with the impression they witnessed the birth of Chinese art and culture, and have seen its beauty,” says Chan. “Maybe they’ll be inspired to acquire some pieces in the future.”
“Engaging Past Wisdom” marks the society’s 65th anniversary and diverges from standard chronological formats by embracing a more thematic curatorial approach. Divided into five chapters and an educational corner that documents the society’s history, the exhibition reflects the breadth and diversity of Chinese arts and antiquities spanning 5,000 years. The exhibits range from calligraphy, jade and bronze to wood furniture and sculptures, fabrics, lacquerware, glassware, weaponry and metalwork.
When I compared the pieces to babies, I was joking, but not really
Maria Mok Kar-wing
The first section, “Preserving the Past: Perceiving the Present”, showcases long-standing motifs in Chinese art. A more than 2,000-year-old vase, or “ritual vessel”, from the Zhou dynasty is placed next to a pale-blue Qing dynasty vase that was made almost two millennia later, mimicking the shape, form and even animal-motif handles of the older one and reflecting the enduring power of techniques and ideologies.
A pendant of a dancing robed woman with one hand on her head and the other on her hip appears behind the vessels. Dating back to the 3rd century BC, she appears to be almost floating. This attention to detail recurs through the displays. “We are really fussy with how we mount the objects,” says Mok.
The second section contains minimalist ceramics, typical of the Song dynasty, whereas the third section sees objects of clothing take centre stage, including a set of ceremonial robes from the Qing dynasty worn by the emperor. Traditionally, the numbers nine and five symbolised the ruler’s supreme authority and, as such, the robe was decorated with five dragons on the front, with an additional three on the back.

The dichotomy between craftsmanship and natural beauty informs the fourth section, where an intricately carved spinach-green jade table screen from the 18th century Qing dynasty depicting a tributary scene, is placed next to an untouched natural Lingbi stone from the county of the same name, in Anhui province. The objects “can be naturalistic, minimalistic or ornate and require a lot of craftsmanship”, says Mok.
One of Chan’s highlights is a Ming dynasty Yongle blue-and-white basin inspired by a Persian original.
The piece’s significance lies in its representation of the Silk Road trade at the time between China and Persia. The vibrancy of the glaze and pigment in particular attracted Chan, and while it took him some time to acquire it, he says he’s never encountered a similar piece on the market.
Another rare work on view, a Wucai covered jar in a “hundred boys” design dating back to the mid-17th century, happens to be the first substantial piece Chan acquired. Adorned with a traditional and auspicious motif that signifies abundance, the jar depicts a colourful and highly detailed scene in which boys are seen engrossed in various recreational activities.

“My wife and I became so fascinated by this piece that ever since we bought it, we have been hooked on collecting antiques,” says Chan, who acquired the jar at a Sotheby’s auction.
While Hong Kong maintains its status in the contemporary art market, it’s exhibitions such as “Engaging Wisdom” that keep the city at the forefront of classical Chinese art and antiquity scholarship.
“Contemporary art comes and goes,” says Chan, emphasising that the Min Chiu Society doesn’t entertain collections or collectors of digital, AI or 21st century art. “All kinds of Chinese antiquities and classical art will always be there, generation after generation …”

data.title: Russian mother warns Malaysians over box jellyfish threat after boy dies from sting
data.description:
A two-year-old Russian boy has died from complications after being stung by a box jellyfish at Chenang Beach in Langkawi, Malaysia’s Kedah state, following five days of fighting for his life.
Sinar Harian reported that Vladimir Lakubanets died on Wednesday at the Sultanah Bahiyah Hospital (HSB) in Alor Setar after suffering serious complications from the sting, which occurred last Saturday.
His father, Nikita, 32, said the incident happened suddenly while his son was playing in shallow water.
“My wife handed my child to me and within seconds, he stopped breathing. I performed CPR as quickly as possible and other tourists helped take us to the beach rescue,” he said.
The rescue team washed the wound with vinegar before taking Lakubanets to a clinic and then to the hospital.
Despite the efforts of medical teams in Langkawi and Alor Setar, Lakubanets was pronounced dead at 9.46am.
Nikita said the family appreciated all the help provided and had no intention of taking legal action, hoping instead that the tragedy serves as a warning to beachgoers about box jellyfish dangers.
His wife, Olga, 32, described Lakubanets as a loving and helpful child, saying, “He is our hero … our little angel. Every day we tell him we love him and he always said he loves us back.”
The couple plan to bring Lakubanets’ ashes back to Russia after cremation.
It was earlier reported that Lakubanets received initial treatment at Sultanah Maliha Hospital in Langkawi before being transferred to the HSB intensive care unit.
Box jellyfish are among the most poisonous marine life, with venom delivered by contact with their tentacles. Their sting can be fatal if not treated immediately.
This story was first published by The Star
